Our architecture is a concrete instantiation of exactly that pattern, and it deliberately extends it in the one dimension that has moved fastest since the pattern was first described: the AI layer on top. And for readers who want to explore how this platform category is developing across the wider market, Vince Kuraitis’s series “Platforming Healthcare – The Long View” offers a thorough long-term perspective.

What binds these layers together is the data product, and it comes in two kinds. Primary data products are sourced directly from a clinical or enterprise application and represent the original, unprocessed data: claims records from the revenue cycle management system, encounter summaries from the EMR, patient records from a clinical data repository (CDR), or financials from SAP Cloud ERP. Derived data products are curated and enriched on top of them, and here, health-specific content such as Patient 360, treatment cost, clinical workforce readiness, and patient risk scores, which deliver ready-to-use insight without additional integration effort, are being built out. Anyone who has run a hospital integration program knows why this matters: instead of maintaining n×m point-to-point integrations between sources and consumers, you build governed, reusable assets once and consume them everywhere, with lineage and policy enforced from the start.

This is what that AI layer changes in practice: because it is grounded in exactly this governed, contextualized data, and because it acts through the platform’s managed capabilities rather than around them, AI can be deployed where it demonstrably relieves pressure in daily operations. SAP Joule, our AI assistant, is being extended by SAP and partners with healthcare scenarios to address the everyday pain points of running a hospital, from clinical documentation, coding and DRG optimization, and denial management to medication replenishment, patient scheduling, regulatory reporting, and workforce planning, while differentiating industry scenarios around Patient 360, clinical workforce optimization, and denial prevention and revenue recovery build on the very same foundation. The CIO needs neither a separate AI stack nor a separate governance regime for it, and no separate integration program to feed it, because the platform itself is the AI foundation. It is also the path to what we at SAP call the Autonomous Enterprise: operations in which routine work increasingly runs itself, under the institution's own rules and controls. That kind of autonomy cannot be installed; it becomes possible only once connected data, business context, and governance are already in place, which is exactly what the layers beneath the AI provide.

That this approach works in practice is not a hypothesis: Hirslanden, Switzerland’s largest private hospital group, modernized its core processes and systems to support new clinical practices, regulatory standards, and cost reimbursement using SAP Signavio business transformation solutions, an example of how process intelligence grounded in a common platform accelerates transformation in healthcare rather than adding to the project load.

An ecosystem by design

No single vendor covers healthcare end to end, and we at SAP do not pretend to. Partners extend the platform with the clinical capabilities and industry solutions, built on SAP Business Technology Platform and SAP Business Data Cloud, delivered through SAP PartnerEdge, and available via SAP Store. For the CIO, that translates into freedom of choice at the edges and a stable, standards-based core in the middle.

A pragmatic sequence, not a big bang

Given the maintenance and regulatory horizons described above, a deliberate sequence is in fact the lower-risk path. It begins with mapping the landscape and agreeing on three to five cross-functional use cases that span clinical and operational domains. It continues with setting up the data foundation and publishing the first data products with governance in place, and with modernizing the ERP core at the institution’s own pace, be it with GROW with SAP for first-time adopters or with RISE with SAP for existing landscapes, where most providers favor separating the ERP and hospital information system transformations, with ERP first, to harmonize master data before clinical systems are subsequently integrated. It then connects the EMR, RCM, and priority partner solutions through standards-based integration including HL7 FHIR®, and it proves value early with time-boxed pilots before scaling responsibly.

The outcome is what the board asked for in the first place, and it is what running best means in healthcare: an institution that adopts innovation at its own speed, meets its regulatory obligations by design, and, where it chooses to, takes part in emerging health data collaborations on its own terms. I will not promise any CIO that a platform shrinks the portfolio overnight, because none does, and every practitioner knows that platforms are additive in the first place before they are consolidating. What it does provide is something more important: an operational model with a credible, governed path from a landscape that dictates its own terms to one that follows yours.

entry.title: Summer Learning Spotlight: Discovering Joule Studio
entry.content:
The summer months can be a great opportunity to step back from daily project work and invest some time in learning something new.

As SAP Business AI continues to evolve, I find it increasingly interesting to explore the technologies that help bring AI closer to business users. One topic that recently caught my attention is Joule Studio.

What I enjoy most about learning is that it doesn't always have to be tied to a certification or a specific project. Sometimes it's simply about discovering a new capability, understanding its potential, and gaining inspiration for future use cases.

That's why Joule Studio is currently on my learning list. If you're looking for a new topic to explore this summer, the following SAP Learning course might be a good starting point:

👉 https://learning.sap.com/courses/introducing-joule-studio

What is currently on your learning list? I'd love to hear which SAP technologies or AI topics you're exploring this summer.

entry.title: Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified - Security Administrator
entry.content:
Security is at the heart of every successful SAP implementation. Behind every secure business process is a solid understanding of authorizations, identity management, and user access.

If you're preparing for the SAP Certified Associate – Security Administrator certification, the Partner Learning Accelerator is designed to help you focus on the topics that matter most. During this 6-hour live session, SAP experts will guide you through core authorization concepts, business role design, SAP Fiori authorizations, identity and access management, and the tools used to administer users and troubleshoot access issues across SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

The thing is, certification preparation is often easier when you can discuss real scenarios, ask questions, and validate what you already know with experienced instructors and fellow partners.

If you've already started your learning journey, this accelerator is a great opportunity to reinforce your knowledge and approach the C_SEC exam with greater confidence.

Register now and take the next step toward your SAP Security Administrator certification. https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jun/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010506.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPSecurity #SAPCertification #S4HANA #IdentityAccessManagement

entry.title: What’s New in SAP Build: Q1 & Q2 2026 Release Highlights
entry.content:
Greetings, Builders! We blinked and just like that, the first half of 2026 is complete. There’s been a whirlwind of updates, including our recent announcement at SAPPHIRE of the new Joule Studio for intent-based development and agent creation.

Q1 and Q2 also brought a wave of innovations across SAP Build, helping our users extend and automate applications faster than ever. From AI-powered capabilities in SAP Build Work Zone, Joule Work Mobile, CAP, and SAPUI5, to improved visibility in process automation and more, these enhancements address some of your key pain points around developer productivity, simplified workflows, and user experience. Here are the Q1 and Q2 2026 release highlights from SAP Build, plus a look ahead at the new Joule Studio and how you can start learning the fundamentals of AI Agent building today.

Improved Customization and Gen AI in SAP Build Work Zone

No, it's not a social networking site from 2003, it's personalized, relevant SAP content.

Easy access to personalized content with My Space

Following the clear feedback from customers, it’s now possible to organize and manage your favorite Work Zone content on a dedicated, personal page. Many business users often have multiple roles, which may include content spanned across various spaces and structures. The “My Space” capability lets you bring all of that together on one page, allowing you to quickly access your relevant content and easily edit it to fit your specific needs.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @ThomasHensel

Enhanced text generation and refinement with AI

The text widget toolbar now includes an AI option to generate text for the widget. In addition, AI can refine the text in input fields, text areas, and text widgets. This includes changing the tone of the text, adjusting its length, and fixing spelling and grammar.

Create whole workspaces, locate relevant content and more with Joule

At the end of last year, Joule became integrated into SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition, bringing the AI assistant’s conversational intelligence right to your workspace. Now, Joule supports three new, frequently used scenarios:

  • Transactional: Create workspaces based on templates, upload content, or invite users to become members.
  • Navigational: Search for specific workspaces that you manage or are related to keywords.
  • Informational: Search for a specific user and show their profile.

You can trigger each of these scenarios through the familiar conversational experience with Joule, executing more tasks and finding key information faster.

👉 See example prompts and learn more on the Help Portal.

👉 See all the latest releases from SAP Build Work Zone.

Updates in Mobile: Seamless SSO, end-user support, MDK 26.6 & more

SAP Mobile Start’s rebranding, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on

SAP Mobile Start has been the established native mobile entry point for your company’s systems and daily tasks, including content from SAP Build Work Zone. The app has now been rebranded as the Joule Work mobile app. While the core functionalities remain the same as before, the new app includes an updated Joule icon, full-screen interface, and voice dictation of prompts. 

With version 3.0, Joule Work mobile app also introduces a streamlined single sign-on for opening web applications in the in-app browser. For organizations with large mobile workforces, this eliminates the need for repetitive credential entry and creates a seamless workflow across applications. Learn more about these updates in the blog posts by @ThiloBerndt and @thomas-bruckner.

  • SAP Mobile Start - Rebranding of the app, enhanced Joule and improved single sign-on functionality
  • Joule Work mobile app version 3.1 is out
  • Seamless Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On with the Joule Work mobile app

Customizing in-app support for end users in Joule Work

Getting technical support for mobile app issues is a common frustration that often causes users to turn to the wrong channels, such as app store reviews or generic feedback forms. It’s now possible for companies to add support information, such as contact details, ticketing systems and resources, directly to your Joule Work mobile app and configure it in SAP Mobile Services. This enhancement transforms how your end users can access help when they need it most.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @ivan_chaushev.

AI feedback forms, extended compliance checks and more in MDK Client 26.6

The latest release of Mobile development kit (MDK) is now available, bringing several significant upgrades to security, compliance and embedded AI in native mobile app development. Device compliance checks have now been extended to before a user onboards, allowing for earlier detection of compromised devices and stronger security. Enhancements to AI are also ongoing, including a new control that enables quick user feedback on AI-generated content.

👉 See the complete list of new MDK features in this blog post by @Jitendra_Kansal.

New CAP plugins for AI recommendations & improved traceability

RPT-1 generated recommendations with CAP AI plugin for node.js 

In pro-code development, SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) continues to provide productivity gains by allowing developers to reduce boilerplate code and focus on problem solving. The new SAP CAP AI plugin for node.js provides recommendations powered by RPT-1 within the in-app UI. The plugin supports multi-tenancy, simplifies AI Core configuration, and inherently embeds the enterprise readiness of CAP out-of-the-box.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @rupamaity.

New and improved change tracking plugin

Version 2.0 of the change tracking plugin for CAP has been released and brings major improvements to how enterprise applications capture and visualize data changes. The new version includes tree table visualization, support for CDS Expression Language (CXL), and a fundamentally redesigned architecture. These changes bring significant upgrades to performance and traceability, allowing developers to exercise more control over changes and customizations.

👉 Learn more in the blog post by @rupamaity.

Streamlined SAPUI5 development with AI and developer extensibility

UI5 plugins for modernization and coding agents in GitHub Copilot

Keeping your SAPUI5 app up to date just got easier. The new UI5 Modernization Plugin automates the most time-consuming parts of app modernization: replacing deprecated APIs, eliminating global namespaces, and much more. The plugin is available for Claude Code as well as GitHub Copilot. Also now available for GitHub Copilot are UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents (previously known as UI5 Plugins for Claude). These bring up-to-date SAPUI5 knowledge directly into your AI-assisted development workflow.

The latest version of the UI5 MCP Server is also now available and supports the development of UI Integration Cards. It includes three new tools to assist Large Language Models (LLMs) in creating new cards from scratch or editing existing ones, following the best practices for card development. Learn more about these plugins:

  • Introducing the UI5 Modernization Plugin: Modernize Your OpenUI5/SAPUI5 App in Hours, Not Weeks
  • UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents: Now Supporting GitHub Copilot
  • UI5 MCP Server Got Extended for UI Integration Cards Generation

Key User Adaptation & Transitions for UI5 TypeScript developers

With key user adaptation, business users can change the UI of SAP Fiori standard apps directly in the runtime app. If more complex changes are needed, there now is a seamless transition from key user to developer extensibility in SAP Build.

TypeScript is going through its biggest transition in years. If you build SAPUI5 applications with TypeScript, we have provided a guide for developers around these changes.

👉 Learn more about key user adaptation and the TypeScript transition.

Extended visibility and proactive monitoring in process automation

Avoid manual error-tracing with automated exception handling.

Enhanced process visibility with instance-level attributes

Operational teams often lack business context in process monitoring. For a business user, this can make it difficult to see key details like approaching deadlines or decisions taken by the previous approver. Now with instance-level attributes enabled, you can add meaningful business context to process instances, allowing users to easily search, filter, group, and analyze processes. This allows teams to improve their decision-making and reduce the risk of breaching SLAs.

👉 Learn more about instance-level attributes in the Help Portal.

Process group connections & other updates

More updates are now available in process automation, focused on improved visibility, proactive monitoring, and enhanced governance. Here are some highlights:

  • Process group connections (1-to-many): Organizations often need visibility across related processes, not just individual workflows. This enables end-to-end monitoring by linking a primary process with its associated downstream processes, providing a more complete view of business execution.
  • Notifications in Visibility Scenarios: Proactive email alerts notify users about processing failures, skipped instances, or event thresholds before they impact operations. This reduces manual monitoring and helps teams respond to issues faster.
  • Environment Filters: New controls allow users to view or restrict process visibility by environment. This improves governance and makes it easier to manage and analyze process data across development, test, and production landscapes.
  • Event handler to catch Action errors: Introduces configurable Retry mechanisms and structured exception handling at the Action level. This allows organizations to systematically manage errors, while reducing manual and time-consuming troubleshooting efforts.

👉 See all the latest releases and documentation on the Help Portal.

Looking ahead to the new Joule Studio

If you're looking to learn more about the new Joule Studio and how you can use it to start creating AI agents, we have great news: the learning journey Introducing Joule Studio is now available! With this free course, you'll be able to understand the bigger picture and how Joule Studio fits into SAP Business AI Platform. You'll also get to know what you can do with Joule Studio and Joule Work, how you can use it to create agents and automate workflows with n8n.

👉 Start learning today: Introducing Joule Studio

entry.title: Export SAP Integration Suite Inventory to Excel: Two Python Scripts for CPI iFlows and API Proxies.
entry.content:
If you have ever been asked how many iFlows sit on a tenant, or which API proxies belong to which product, you already know how it goes. You open the UI, click into one package at a time, and copy names into a spreadsheet by hand. It takes a while, and the list is out of date the moment someone adds a new flow. I got tired of doing this, so I wrote a couple of small scripts to do it for me.

There are two scripts. Each one reads a different part of SAP Integration Suite and writes the result straight into an Excel file. They use the standard public OData APIs with an OAuth client credential, so nothing is installed on the tenant. You run them from your own machine and you get a clean spreadsheet at the end.

Here is the one thing to keep straight while you read. There are two separate scripts for two separate jobs:

  • cpi_inventory_export.py reads Cloud Integration and lists every integration package and the iFlows inside each one.
  • apim_inventory_export.py reads API Management and lists every API proxy and the product it belongs to.

They look alike and run the same way, but they connect to different services in BTP and need different roles. To avoid any mix up, I have written each one as its own part below. The download link for both is at the end.

What each script produces

Script Reads from Output file Sheets you get

cpi_inventory_export.pyCloud IntegrationCPI_Inventory.xlsxPackages, iFlows
apim_inventory_export.pyAPI ManagementAPIM_Inventory.xlsxAPI Proxies, Products

The output is plain and easy to filter. The CPI file gives you one row per iFlow with its package name, so you can sort or pivot however you like. The APIM file gives you one row per proxy with the product it sits in, which is the view most people are really after.

Before you start

A few things need to be in place. These are the same for both scripts.

  • You can log in to the SAP BTP cockpit for your subaccount.
  • Cloud Integration or API Management is active in your Integration Suite tenant, depending on which script you want to run.
  • Python 3 is installed on your machine. You can check by opening a Command Prompt and running the command below.
python --version

If you see a version number come back, you are good. If the command is not recognised, install Python from python.org and tick the option to add it to PATH during setup.

Both scripts also need two small Python libraries. You only install these once, and the same two work for both scripts.

python -m pip install requests openpyxl

Part 1: Cloud Integration packages and iFlows (cpi_inventory_export.py)

This is the script that answers the question, what packages and iFlows do we have. It talks to the Process Integration Runtime service.

Step 1: Create the service instance

In your subaccount, open Service Marketplace and search for Process Integration Runtime. Open the tile and choose Create. On the Basic Info screen set these values:

  • Plan: api. This one matters. The integration-flow plan is only for sending messages to a deployed iFlow, and it will not list your packages. If you pick the wrong plan you get a 404 later.
  • Runtime Environment: Cloud Foundry
  • Space: your space, for example dev
  • Instance Name: cpi-extract

Step 2: Add the role

Move to the Parameters step. In the Roles field select WorkspacePackagesRead and keep the grant type as Client Credentials. This role is what lets the script read your packages and iFlows. If you leave it out, the API later tells you it cannot find the entity set, which is a confusing error for what is really just a missing role. Choose Create.

Step 3: Create the service key

Open the cpi-extract instance, go to Service Keys, and create one. Name it something like cpi-extract-key and leave the parameters box as the empty braces. Open the key in JSON view and copy these four values:

  • url
  • tokenurl
  • clientid
  • clientsecret

The Cloud Integration url usually has it-cpi in the host name and often ends with -rt. Make a mental note of that, because it is the easiest way to tell it apart from the API Management url in Part 2.

Step 4: Fill in the script and run it

Open cpi_inventory_export.py in any editor. Near the top, paste your four values inside the quotes and save. Keep the quotes in place.

CLIENT_ID = "your clientid"
CLIENT_SECRET = "your clientsecret"
TOKEN_URL = "your tokenurl"
API_URL = "your url"

Open a Command Prompt, move into the folder that holds the script, and run it.

cd "C:\path\to\your\folder"
python cpi_inventory_export.py

When it finishes you will see a line like Done. 12 packages exported to CPI_Inventory.xlsx. Open the file and you will find a Packages sheet and an iFlows sheet, sitting in the same folder as the script.

Part 2: API Management proxies and products (apim_inventory_export.py)

This is the script that answers the question, what API proxies do we have and which product is each one in. It talks to the API Management, API portal service, which is a different service from Part 1.

Step 1: Create the service instance

In your subaccount, open Service Marketplace and search for API Management, API portal. Take care to pick this tile and not the developer portal tile next to it. Open it and choose Create. On the Basic Info screen set these values:

  • Plan: apiportal-apiaccess
  • Runtime Environment: Cloud Foundry
  • Space: your space, for example dev
  • Instance Name: api-extract

 

Step 2: Add the role

Move to the Parameters step. Here the role is entered as JSON, not picked from a list. This is the step people most often miss, and skipping it is what leaves you with an empty service key later. Type the role, then choose Create.

{ "role": "APIPortal.Administrator" }

Do not have the Administrator role? You do not need it for this. The Administrator role allows create, update, and delete, but this script only reads. If you cannot or should not use Administrator, use the read-only role instead:

{ "role": "APIPortal.Guest" }

APIPortal.Guest gives read-only access to the API portal, which is all the script needs to list your proxies and products. It is also the safer choice if you are following least privilege, so if you are unsure, start with Guest. Whichever role you choose, it goes here on the Parameters step of the instance, not on the service key.

Step 3: Create the service key

Open the api-extract instance, go to Service Keys, and create one. Name it something like api-extract-key and leave the parameters box as the empty braces. Open the key in JSON view and copy these four values:

  • url
  • tokenUrl
  • clientId
  • clientSecret

The API Management url has the word apiportal in the host name. This is the biggest difference from Part 1. If you accidentally use the Cloud Integration host, the one with it-cpi, you will get a 404, so double check you copied the url from this key and not the CPI one.

Step 4: Fill in the script and run it

Open apim_inventory_export.py in any editor. Near the top, paste your four values inside the quotes and save. Keep the quotes in place.

CLIENT_ID = "your clientId"
CLIENT_SECRET = "your clientSecret"
TOKEN_URL = "your tokenUrl"
API_URL = "your url"

Open a Command Prompt, move into the folder that holds the script, and run it.

cd "C:\path\to\your\folder"
python apim_inventory_export.py

When it finishes you will see a line like Done. 1 proxies and 1 products exported to APIM_Inventory.xlsx. Open the file and you will find an API Proxies sheet and a Products sheet.

Quick comparison of the two scripts

If you take away one thing from this blog, take away this table. It shows exactly where the two scripts differ, so you always know which value belongs to which script.

  cpi_inventory_export.py apim_inventory_export.py

Service in BTPProcess Integration RuntimeAPI Management, API portal
Planapiapiportal-apiaccess
RoleWorkspacePackagesReadAPIPortal.Administrator, or APIPortal.Guest for read-only
url containsit-cpiapiportal
Output fileCPI_Inventory.xlsxAPIM_Inventory.xlsx

Common issues

These are the problems you are most likely to hit, with the quick fix for each.

  • Service key is empty. The role was not set on the instance. Recreate the instance with the role, then make a new key. For API Management, remember you can use APIPortal.Guest if Administrator is not available.
  • 404 not found. Wrong host or wrong plan. Use the url from the correct service key, and check the plan.
  • Entity set not found on CPI. Add WorkspacePackagesRead and create a new key.
  • 401 unauthorized. One of the four values is mistyped. Copy them again from the key.
  • No module named requests. Run the pip install command again.
  • SyntaxError when you run the command. You are inside the Python shell, shown by three greater than signs. Type exit() first, then run the command from the normal prompt.

Download the scripts

Both scripts are on GitHub with a short README. Download them here and follow along.

Download: cpi_inventory_export.py

Download: apim_inventory_export.py

If this saved you some clicking, drop a comment and tell me what you would like to see next. I am happy to extend the scripts, for example to add deployment status or the last changed date for each artifact..

entry.title: reCAP 2026 – Recordings of our Annual CAP Developer Conference
entry.content:
On 15 July 2026, the CAP community came together for reCAP 2026 in St. Leon-Rot. Again, tickets were sold out within just a few minutes. We had more than 400 participants onsite and many more joining online.

For everyone who couldn’t attend live, most sessions were recorded. Below is a summary of the recorded sessions, along with links to the recordings. You can find all recordings on YouTube.


 

Opening & Keynote

Intro by Ole Lilienthal
Setting the stage, Ole gave an overview of the Autonomous Enterprise and how CAP plays a key role in enabling the SAP Business AI platform as the underlying platform for the Autonomous Enterprise. He also explained the importance of CAP in the age of AI.  

Recording link

Keynote by Daniel Hutzel
In his keynote, Daniel shared an overview of the biggest CAP achievements since the last reCAP, including AI-related topics.

Recording link

 

Main Track Sessions in Audimax

CAP Tools – What’s New and Hot by Christian Georgi
A tour through the latest and greatest in CAP tooling, including:

  • `cds repl` with queries
  • Debugging apps on Kyma
  • CDS Project outline in Visual Studio Code
  • CAP in browser

Recording link

Optimizing CAP Performance with Data Replication and Caching – Part II by Mike Zaschka
As a follow-up to last year’s talk on cds-caching, this session broadened the view to the full landscape of caching, replication, and federation strategies in the CAP ecosystem. Mike walked through a curated selection of solutions and offered practical guidance on which approach fits which scenario.

Recording link

AI-Assisted Development of CAP Apps by Daniel Schlachter and Stefan Rudi
A look at the CAP MCP development server, which gives AI coding assistants direct access to CAP documentation improving code quality in tools like Claude Code and GitHub Copilot.

Recording link

Expressions and Abstractions – Two Developer Superpowers in CAP by Patrice Bender and DJ Adams
A demonstration of two powerful CAP affordances: expressions (what they are, how to use them, and why they matter) and mocking (letting you iterate fast on data, auth, messaging, and remote services).

Recording link

CAP Plugins: Latest and Greatest by Max Eckert, Dries Van Vaerenbergh, Matthis Vansteenhuyse, and Lisa Julia Nebel
CAP plugins dramatically simplify integration with SAP BTP services. This session demystified the plugin model and spotlighted the enhanced attachments plugin, the renovated change tracking plugin, a new notifications plugin for Java, and the new SAP Build Process Automation plugin.

Recording link

Plug, Connect, Play: CAP Integration Made Effortless by Ram Prasad GS and Bilal El-Massoudi
An introduction to cap-connect, a production-ready, open-source CAP plugin from Aarini Consulting that brings first-class connection management to any CAP application. The talk also showcased how cap-connect powers Prodsphere, Aarini’s own CAP-based product.

Recording link

Production GenAI in CAP: Beyond the Demo by Moudhaffer Azizi
This session explored the architecture behind a production-grade CAP application on SAP BTP that uses RAG to analyse custom ABAP code against Clean Core standards. The session highlighted the journey from a single prompt to a resilient solution orchestrating 14 AI-powered operations.

Recording link

Beyond Vibe Coding: Engineering Deterministic CAP Applications with SDD by Maximilian Hartig and Abdulbasıt Gülşen
A live "0-to-1" walkthrough of Spec-Driven Development using the open-source Spec Kit in Visual Studio Code to transform a raw business requirement into a CAP application.

Recording link

From Click-Ops to Code-Ops (Terraform) by Nicholas Arefta
Nicholas demonstrated how to use Terraform to deploy multitenant apps on SAP BTP. He demonstrated provisioning of a Provider subaccount, deploying a multi-tenant application and onboarding a Subscriber completely hands-free.

Recording link

Custom Business Logic with Predefined Extension Points – Using CDS-Oyster by Nick Josipovic and Konrad Koschel
Together with Subscription Billing and Cloud Foundation, Nick and Konrad built an extensive Extension Box using CAP and the CDS-Oyster Code Sandbox enabling secure code extensions in a complex cloud environment.

Recording link

Implementing Runtime Plugins in CAP Java by Joshua Mitchell
Joshua demonstrated in his live session how to program a basic soft delete plugin for CAP Java using annotations, the IntelliJ debugger, and an expression evaluator.

Recording link

 

Side Track Sessions in W1/W2

Building Code-Based AI Agents with CAP and UI5: From Concept to Production by Wouter Lemaire

Using the Smart Monitoring Agent (previously demonstrated at SAP TechEd) as a real-world example, Wouter showed how to build intelligent, code-based AI agents by combining SAP AI Core with CAP and UI5. The session covered the complete journey from development to deployment.

Recording link

Building AI-enabled Applications with CAP by Max Eckert, Simon Engel, Markus Riedinger, and Peter W. Szabo

An overview of how CAP is evolving to meet the AI era including: exposing CAP services as endpoints for AI agents, leveraging the CAP AI plugin, exploring additional features, and building intelligent, code-driven agents.

Recording link

Building a CAP Application with Latest Best Practices and AI by Lukas Theis, Johannes Vogt, Marc Becker, and Robin de Silva Jayasinghe

A practical, end-to-end walkthrough from idea to production-ready CAP app, combining proven CAP architecture patterns with AI-assisted workflows.

Recording link

Declarative Programming Patterns in CAP - Less Code, More Power by Adrian Görler, Robin de Silva Jayasinghe, and Vitaly Kozyura

A look at how declarative mechanisms in CAP such as calculated elements, annotation-based constraints (e.g. @assert, @assert.range, @assert.format), and field control help to reduce custom code. The session also covered how status transition flows help reducing the need for custom validation logic while ensuring data integrity.

Recording link

Update from the cap-js-community - Experience the latest additions to CAP by Oliver Klemenz

A focused technical update on the latest enhancements in the cap-js-community, with a deep dive into the WebSocket modules and the newly introduced common utilities module.

Recording link

AI-Assisted Security Hardening of CAP Applications by Matthias Braun

Matthias showed how AI-powered tools can help identify authorization gaps, generate comprehensive security tests, and detect attack vectors. This significantly reduces the effort required to build and maintain secure CAP applications.

Recording link

Event Queues Scheduling by Sebastian Van Syckel, Dietrich Mostowoj, Thomas Bonk, and Lars Plessing
A deep dive into Transactional Event Queues, which allow scheduling of events and background tasks for asynchronous, exactly-once processing with strong resilience.

Recording link

Automated Testing with HANA Cloud by Armin Hatting
Armin presented practical approaches to integrating database-supported testing in CAP applications, setting up realistic test environments, and ensuring the functionality of SAP HANA Cloud native logic.

Recording link

Did you know about infix filters and projection functions? by Nico Schoenteich

A live-coding overview of infix filters and projection functions (Node.js) and how they were applied in a recent project to reduce custom service.

Recording link

 

Thank You

A huge thank-you to all speakers, attendees, and community members who made reCAP 2026 such an inspiring day. 🙌 The energy, the ideas, and the conversations are exactly what makes the CAP community special.

If you missed any session, use the recording links above to catch up.

We look forward to seeing how you use what you learned at reCAP 2026. If you have any feedback, please let us know.

Happy coding! 😊

entry.title: Groovy in your SAP Cloud Integration iFlows is application code. Here is what to check.
entry.content:
As PI/PO approaches the end of mainstream maintenance in 2027, most of us are moving
integration onto SAP Integration Suite / Cloud Integration. In the process, a lot of
custom logic ends up in Groovy script steps. And a Groovy script step is not a mapping
expression: it is full application code running on the tenant. It can run OS commands,
open sockets, read the filesystem and handle credentials.

That makes iFlow scripts a real security surface, and one that most SAP security reviews
still skip. Below are the patterns worth checking in your own script steps, with small
examples.

1. OS command execution from message data

A classic mistake is building a shell command out of a header or payload field:

def name = message.getHeaders().get("X-Document-Name")
def out = ["/bin/sh", "-c", "process " + name].execute().text

If `X-Document-Name` is attacker controlled, so is the command. Avoid `.execute()`,
`Runtime.exec` and `ProcessBuilder` on anything derived from the message. If you truly
need a process call, pass a fixed argument array and validate every value against an
allow list.

2. XXE in XML parsing

`XmlSlurper` and `XmlParser` resolve DTDs and external entities by default:

def xml = new XmlSlurper().parse(
new ByteArrayInputStream(message.getBody(byte[].class)))

A crafted payload can read local files or reach internal endpoints. Disable DTDs and
external entities explicitly:

def parser = new XmlSlurper()
parser.setFeature("http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl", true)
parser.setFeature("http://xml.org/sax/features/external-general-entities", false)

3. Path traversal on file names

def name = message.getHeaders().get("X-Document-Name")
new File("/tmp/inbound/" + name).text = body

A value like `../../somewhere` breaks out of the intended folder. Normalize the path and
confirm it still sits under the base directory before writing.

4. Hardcoded secrets and disabled TLS checks

Credentials pasted into a script step, or a client that turns off certificate or hostname
verification to "make it work", both survive far longer than anyone intends. Use the
Security Material store for secrets, and never disable TLS verification in production
scripts.

5. Sensitive data in the message log

messageLog?.setStringProperty("payload", message.getBody(String.class))

Convenient for debugging, but full payloads can carry PII, tokens or bank data straight
into logs that a wider group can read. Log identifiers, not bodies.

Why this matters

None of this is exotic. It is the same class of issue we already scan for in custom ABAP,
just moved to a new runtime and a new language. The problem is that it is spread across
many iFlows and rarely reviewed line by line.

We built a scanner for exactly this at RedRays. It connects to your Integration Suite
runtime, pulls the iFlow content and statically analyzes each Groovy, JavaScript and XSLT
script step for the issues above, returning a CVSS score and the exact file and line, in
the same console as our ABAP scans.

> **Disclosure:** I work at RedRays, so treat this paragraph as vendor context, not a
> neutral recommendation. The checklist above stands on its own and you can apply it by
> hand or with any tool you prefer.

If you have run into other risky Groovy patterns in CPI, I would like to hear them in the
comments so we can build a fuller list for the community.

entry.title: From Whiteboard to SAP BTP: Building an Open Source AI Architecture Diagram Generator
entry.content:
Every SAP BTP architect knows the ritual. You finish a whiteboard session, photograph the sketch on your phone, and then spend the next hour manually recreating it in draw.io — hunting through the SAP icon library, wiring up the right service boxes, getting the boundary zones right. The diagram ends up taking longer than the actual architecture decision.

We built a tool to fix that. Sketch to SAP BTP takes a whiteboard photo or a Mermaid diagram and converts it into an official SAP BTP Solution Diagram in .drawio format — complete with the right SAP service icons, boundary zones, and connector styles. It runs in seconds, on your own BTP subaccount, using your own Anthropic API key.

Today we're releasing it as open source under Apache 2.0.

The tool UI — file upload tab and Mermaid input tab side by side

What it does

The tool accepts two kinds of input:

  • Whiteboard sketch — a photo (PNG, JPEG, WebP, GIF up to 10 MB) of a hand-drawn architecture
  • Mermaid diagram — any flowchart or graph definition

From either input, it runs a four-step pipeline:

Identify components → Map to SAP catalog → Review architecture → Generate .drawio

The output is a ready-to-open .drawio file with proper SAP service icons, swimlane boundary zones, and styled connectors. Drop it into diagrams.net or the draw.io desktop app and it opens correctly without any manual adjustment.

The technical implementation

Step 1 — Claude Vision extracts the architecture

For sketch input, we use Claude's vision capability with structured tool use. Rather than asking Claude to describe the image in natural language and then parsing that output, we define a tool schema that forces the model to return a structured JSON representation of the architecture directly.

// srv/agents/vision.js — simplified
const tools = [{
  name: "extract_architecture",
  input_schema: {
    type: "object",
    properties: {
      title: { type: "string" },
      components: {
        type: "array",
        items: {
          type: "object",
          properties: {
            id: { type: "string" },
            label: { type: "string" },
            type: { type: "string" }
          }
        }
      },
      connections: { /* ... */ }
    }
  }
}];

This approach eliminates prompt-parsing fragility. The model either calls the tool with valid structured data or it doesn't — there's no regex or text parsing required on our side. Claude Sonnet 4.6 with vision handles even rough whiteboard photos reliably.

A whiteboard sketch being uploaded

Step 2 — Mermaid gets a dedicated parser (no LLM needed)

When the input is a Mermaid diagram, we skip the vision model entirely. Mermaid is structured text — parsing it deterministically is faster, cheaper, and more accurate than asking an LLM to interpret it. Our parser handles:

  • All flowchart directions: LR, TD, RL, BT
  • Edge types: -->, -->|label|, -.->, ==>
  • Chained edges: A --> B --> C
  • Cylinder shape [(label)] for databases
  • YAML front-matter and %% title: for diagram titles

Mermaid parsing costs exactly $0 and returns results in milliseconds.

Step 3 — Deterministic SAP service catalog mapping

Once we have a list of labelled components, we map each one to an official SAP BTP service using sap_service_catalog.json — a bundled catalog of SAP services with their draw.io style metadata. Matching runs in three tiers:

Mapping confidence tiers Exact match — the label directly matches a service name or alias (e.g. "SAP HANA Cloud" → hana-cloud)
Alias match — a known abbreviation or alternative name matches (e.g. "HANA" → hana-cloud)
Type fallback — no match found, but the component type (database, queue, API) suggests a generic placeholder

This is all deterministic code — no LLM involved in the mapping step. It's fast, predictable, and easy to extend by adding entries to sap_service_catalog.json.

Step 4 — Architecture review with Claude Haiku

Before generating the final diagram, we pass the mapped architecture to Claude Haiku 4.5 for a lightweight best-practices review. It checks for common issues — missing integration layers, direct database access without a service tier, absent authentication components — and returns structured warnings.

We use Haiku for this step deliberately: the review prompt is short and the expected output is structured JSON warnings. Using a smaller model here keeps the cost per run under a cent while still catching real architectural issues.

The validation results are available two ways: embedded in the downloaded .drawio response as HTTP headers (X-Validation-Summary, X-Warning-Count, X-Missing-Services), or as a standalone JSON response via POST /validate-only if you want the review without generating a diagram.

The validation panel showing detected SAP components with confidence chips and architecture review warnings

Step 5 — draw.io XML generation

The final step converts the mapped, validated architecture into draw.io XML. SAP publishes an official icon set for BTP services — we bundle these as base64 SVG in sap_icon_index.json and embed them directly into the draw.io cell styles. The generator places components in boundary zones (Integration Suite, Extension Suite, etc.) based on service category, positions them using a topology-aware BFS layout — components are assigned columns based on their graph depth from actor/entry-point nodes, preserving the left-to-right data flow of the original sketch — and wires up connectors.

The output is standard draw.io XML — it opens in diagrams.net, the draw.io desktop app, or any tool that reads the format.

The generated .drawio file open in diagrams.net showing SAP service icons, boundary zones, and connectors

API key resolution — three sources, one priority order

The app resolves the Anthropic API key from these sources in priority order:

Source When used
VCAP_SERVICES.aicoreSAP AI Core / GenAI Hub binding
VCAP_SERVICES.credential-storeBTP Credential Store — recommended for production
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env varLocal dev and quick CF deployments

This means the tool works with SAP's own AI Core infrastructure for organisations already using GenAI Hub, while remaining simple to run locally with a direct API key.

Cost per run

Step Model Approx. cost
Sketch analysisclaude-sonnet-4-6~$0.013
Architecture reviewclaude-haiku-4-5~$0.003
Mermaid parsingNo LLM$0.00

All costs are charged to your own Anthropic account — Aditheos has zero visibility into your usage or spend.

Why we open sourced it

The honest answer: a commercial SaaS model doesn't fit this tool well.

Enterprise SAP architects handle sensitive network topology and system landscape data. They will deploy an open source tool to their own private BTP space without hesitation. Getting the same data approved to flow through an external SaaS is a legal review and a procurement process. The paywall would block the exact people the tool is most useful to.

There's also the catalog maintenance problem. SAP's BTP service catalog changes constantly — new services, renamed services, deprecated plans. If this were a closed product, we'd be solely responsible for keeping sap_service_catalog.json up to date. Open source means the community can submit pull requests when a service launches or changes. That's a better outcome for everyone.

The most impactful contribution Adding new entries to data/sap_service_catalog.json — service names, aliases, draw.io style metadata — directly improves mapping accuracy for every user. If your organisation uses a service that isn't mapping correctly, a PR to the catalog is the highest-leverage fix.

Getting started in under five minutes

Local development:

git clone https://github.com/aditheos/sketch-to-sap-btp-cap.git
cd sketch-to-sap-btp-cap

cp .env.example .env
# Add your ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to .env

npm install
npm run dev   # → http://localhost:4004

Deploy to your own BTP subaccount:

npm install
npm run build

cf login -a <your-api-endpoint>
cf target -o <your-org> -s <your-space>
cf deploy sketch-to-sap-btp_*.mtar

cf set-env sketch-to-sap-btp-srv ANTHROPIC_API_KEY sk-ant-...
cf restage sketch-to-sap-btp-srv

No additional BTP services required. The deployment is a single Cloud Foundry app. No HANA, no approuter, no IAS — just the CAP backend serving the UI and handling conversions.

What's next

A few directions the community could take this:

  • SAP BAS extension — a plugin that runs the conversion inside Business Application Studio, where architects actually spend their time
  • Two-way generation — from diagram to CAP/BTP project scaffold (the reverse direction)
  • More output formats — PowerPoint-ready slides, Confluence diagrams, PlantUML
  • Expanded catalog — deeper coverage of SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build, and industry-specific services

The repository is at github.com/aditheos/sketch-to-sap-btp-cap. Issues and pull requests are open
Source Reference: Sketch to SAP BTP Diagram

entry.title: Governing the End-to-End Service Cycle in SAP S/4HANA Service with SAP Joule Agents
entry.content:
Introduction

Service is one of the most demanding business processes in SAP S/4HANA.

Every customer interaction can turn into a Service Request, a Service Quotation, a Service Order, and eventually a Service Confirmation — each with its own mandatory data, its own upstream dependency, and its own point at which mistakes become expensive.

Even with modern Fiori experiences, service teams still spend a large part of their day navigating between apps, resolving Business Partners, checking statuses, and validating whether upstream documents are actually ready. When something is missed, the cost is not just rework — it is billing errors, compliance gaps, and eroded customer trust.

With the arrival of SAP Joule, SAP Joule Studio, and the Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Core, we now have a governed way to bring intelligent, conversational experiences directly into these processes — without stepping outside SAP's business rules.

This blog walks through three tightly integrated SAP Joule Agents that together govern the complete service cycle in SAP S/4HANA — a Service Intake Assistant, a Service Order Assistant, and a Service Confirmation Assistant — and shares the design principles and lessons learned from building them.

If you are new to SAP Joule Studio or the Generative AI Hub, the SAP resources linked at the end of this blog are a good place to start before diving in.

The Business Challenge:

Service organizations across Industrial Equipment, Engineering Services, Field Service, and Utilities consistently report the same pain points across the service cycle:

  • Manual navigation and fragmented validation during intake
  • Ambiguous Business Partner resolution leading to downstream billing issues
  • Service Orders created without valid upstream commercial references
  • Premature order releases and uncontrolled updates
  • Planned values reused as actuals during confirmation
  • Irreversible service closure executed without readiness checks
  • Missing audit trail across intake, order, and confirmation stages

The net result: slower cycle times, higher operational risk, weaker first-time-right rates, and financial data that Finance and Analytics cannot fully trust.

What service teams need is not another chatbot — it is a governed, conversational execution layer that respects SAP's business rules at every step of the cycle.

The Three Service Management Agents:

Three SAP Joule Agents, one governed lifecycle.

Figure 1 — End-to-end Service Management cycle governed by the three SAP Joule Agents: Service Intake Assistant, Service Order Assistant, and Service Confirmation Assistant. Every stage follows the same pattern — natural language in, governed validation, backend-confirmed outcome.

1. Service Intake Assistant:

The starting point of the cycle. This agent delivers a governed, AI-assisted intake experience for Service Requests and Service Quotations. It resolves Business Partners from SAP master data, validates mandatory intake information in a single consolidated step, and creates or updates service documents only after successful SAP backend execution — ensuring first-time-right intake and eliminating incomplete service documents at the source.

Because the Service Intake Assistant is the entry point to the entire service cycle, the following walkthrough illustrates its behavior in depth. The same conversational, consolidated-validation, backend-confirmed pattern is then applied consistently by the Service Order Assistant and the Service Confirmation Assistant across the rest of the lifecycle.

 

 

Screenshot 1 — Launching the Service Intake Assistant inside SAP Joule for a natural-language conversation with a service user.

The interaction begins with a simple natural-language request from the service user. The agent then resolves the Business Partner directly against SAP master data and retrieves the partner's existing service documents before creating anything new.

Screenshot 2 — The Service Intake Assistant resolves the Business Partner against SAP master data and retrieves all existing Service Requests for the partner before any new document is created.

Once the Business Partner is confirmed, the agent gathers every mandatory field in a single consolidated interaction rather than a long back-and-forth. If anything is missing, ambiguous, or blocking, the agent communicates it transparently along with what has already been collected — nothing is assumed, nothing is fabricated.

 

Screenshot 3 — Consolidated single-step validation in action: the agent displays every field already available and transparently flags the single missing mandatory input before proceeding to backend execution.

Only when all validations pass does the agent invoke the corresponding SAP backend operation. Success is confirmed only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid document number — never before.

 

 

Screenshot 4 — Backend-confirmed Service Request creation in SAP S/4HANA Service Management — the agent confirms success only after the SAP backend returns a valid document number.

The same governed pattern applies to Service Quotations, which the agent creates against the confirmed Business Partner with full pricing, quantity, validity, and status returned directly from SAP.

 

Screenshot 5 — Backend-confirmed Service Quotation creation for the same Business Partner, with pricing, quantity, validity, and status returned directly from SAP S/4HANA.

Updates to existing service documents follow the same discipline. The agent applies the change only after backend confirmation and presents a clear before-and-after view so that the update is auditable.

 

Screenshot 6 — Controlled update of an existing Service Quotation, with the agent showing the previous and new values only after backend confirmation of the update. 

2. Service Order Assistant:

The execution governance layer of the cycle. This agent controls Service Order creation, updates, and release. It ensures that Service Orders are created only from valid upstream Service Quotation references, enforces consolidated dependency validation, and executes release only when SAP backend conditions are met — eliminating unauthorized or orphan Service Orders and preventing premature releases.

The following walkthrough illustrates the Service Order lifecycle end-to-end: retrieve, create, update, and release.

 

Screenshot 7 — Launching the Service Order Assistant inside SAP Joule, which supports the complete Service Order lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and release.

Before any Service Order is created, the agent enforces its most important governance rule — the presence of a valid upstream Service Quotation reference. Only after the Quotation is confirmed does the agent request the mandatory fields for Service Order creation.

 

Screenshot 8 — Upstream dependency enforcement: the Service Order Assistant validates the referenced Service Quotation before collecting the mandatory fields required for Service Order creation.

When all validations pass, the agent executes the create operation and confirms success only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Order number, linked back to the originating Quotation for traceability.

 

Screenshot 9 — Backend-confirmed Service Order creation, linked to the upstream Service Quotation. Success is confirmed only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Order number.

Updates to existing Service Orders follow the same discipline. The agent applies changes only after backend confirmation and presents a clear before-and-after view for auditability.

 

Screenshot 10 — Controlled item-level update of the Service Order, with the agent showing previous and new values only after successful backend confirmation.

Release is treated as a distinct governance event. Before any release is executed, the agent runs a full Release Readiness Assessment — verifying every prerequisite and reporting blockers transparently. Release is permitted only when the assessment returns clean.

 

Screenshot 11 — Release Readiness Assessment: the Service Order Assistant verifies every release prerequisite (existence, status, upstream reference, organizational data, service details) and reports "no blockers detected" before any release is executed.

Once released, the Service Order is officially available for work scheduling and execution — which is the natural handoff cue to the Service Confirmation Assistant, the third agent in the cycle.

 

Screenshot 12 — Backend-confirmed release of the Service Order in SAP S/4HANA. The agent confirms release status, pricing document creation, and execution readiness only after successful backend validation — completing the governed handoff into service execution.

3. Service Confirmation Assistant:

The closure layer of the cycle. This agent governs actuals-based Service Confirmation creation, updates, and final completion. It ensures that confirmations are recorded only against valid, released Service Orders, enforces a clear separation between planned and actual execution data, and performs final completion only after SAP backend confirmation — treating closure as the irreversible business action it is.

The following walkthrough illustrates the complete Service Confirmation lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and complete.

 

Screenshot 13 — Launching the Service Confirmation Assistant inside SAP Joule. The agent supports the complete Service Confirmation lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and — as an explicitly irreversible action — complete.

Before any confirmation is created, the agent verifies the upstream Service Order's release status and reports whether confirmations already exist. This is the discipline that keeps actuals aligned to real, released work.

 

Screenshot 14 — Before any confirmation is created, the agent retrieves the upstream Service Order's release status and reports the existing confirmation state — enforcing the rule that actuals can only be recorded against valid, released Service Orders.

Once readiness is confirmed, the agent gathers every mandatory field in a single consolidated request — the same design pattern applied by the Intake and Order Assistants.

 

Screenshot 15 — Consolidated mandatory field collection for Service Confirmation creation — the same single-step validation pattern applied by every agent in the Service Management set.

The agent then executes the create operation and confirms success only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Confirmation ID, linked back to the released Service Order for full traceability.

 

Screenshot 16 — Backend-confirmed Service Confirmation creation in SAP S/4HANA, linked back to the released Service Order. Success is confirmed only after the SAP backend returns a valid Service Confirmation ID.

Updates continue to follow the same auditable discipline as the previous agents in the cycle — with a clean before-and-after view after every backend-confirmed change.

 

Screenshot 17 — Controlled update of an existing Service Confirmation, with the agent presenting previous and new values only after backend confirmation — the same auditability pattern applied across the cycle.

Finally, completion is treated as the irreversible business action that it is. The agent captures actual service duration and start time, performs final completion only after SAP backend confirmation, and explicitly communicates that the confirmation is finalized and cannot be undone.

Screenshot 18 — Irreversible Service Confirmation completion in SAP S/4HANA. The agent records actual service duration and start time, finalizes the confirmation as an audit-ready, non-reversible business action, and closes the end-to-end service cycle that began with the Service Intake Assistant.

How the Agents Work Together:

The Service Intake walkthrough above illustrated a design pattern that all three agents share: natural language in, governed SAP execution out.

The Service Order Assistant applies the same discipline to Service Order creation, updates, and release — refusing to create orders without a valid upstream Service Quotation reference, enforcing consolidated dependency validation, and releasing only when SAP backend conditions are met.

The Service Confirmation Assistant closes the loop with the same rigor — recording actual only against released Service Orders, separating planned and actual data cleanly, and performing final closure only after SAP backend confirmation.

The result is a service lifecycle in which no stage can be skipped, and no stage can be executed without the previous one being genuinely valid — exactly what enterprise-grade service governance requires.

 

Figure 2 — Shared design pattern across all three agents: user intent expressed in natural language, consolidated context and validation, governed SAP backend execution, and confirmed outcome that hands off cleanly to the next stage of the service lifecycle.

Solution Architecture:

All three agents are built entirely on SAP's AI and application stack.

Figure 3 — Technical realization on SAP Business Technology Platform: SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Build Process Automation, SAP Joule Studio, SAP AI Core, and SAP Cloud Identity Services deployed in a multi-cloud subaccount, connected to on-premises SAP S/4HANA or SAP ECC through Cloud Connector via OData/REST and HTTPS.

Architecture Components:

  • SAP Joule — Conversational interaction layer for service users.
  • SAP Joule Studio — Design, configure, and orchestrate each agent, its skills, guardrails, and prompts.
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) — Runtime, identity, and extensibility foundation shared across the agent set.
  • Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Core — Governed access to large language models, prompt orchestration, and AI governance.
  • SAP S/4HANA Service Management — System of record for Business Partners, Service Requests, Service Quotations, Service Orders, and Service Confirmations.

Business Value:

Adopting the three Service Management agents together delivers value that no single-point automation can match:

  • First-time-right service intake — Governed data collection and Business Partner resolution eliminate incomplete Service Requests and Quotations at the source.
  • Controlled, traceable execution — Every Service Order is created and released from a valid upstream reference, preventing unauthorized or orphan orders.
  • Accurate, irreversible closure — Confirmations capture real actuals against released orders, protecting billing accuracy and audit readiness.
  • Faster cycle times — Removing manual navigation and fragmented validation shortens intake, execution, and closure across the lifecycle.
  • Trustworthy service data — Finance and Analytics receive service data that reflects real backend execution, not optimistic UI states.
  • Reduced operational risk — Governance is enforced by design across every stage, not left to individual user discipline.

Example Use Cases:

  • A Service Desk Operator captures a customer request and creates a Service Request through a natural-language conversation, without navigating any Fiori app.
  • A Service Order Coordinator converts an approved Service Quotation into a controlled Service Order and releases it once all dependencies are satisfied.
  • A Field Service Technician records actual time and effort against a released Service Order and closes the service as a final, irreversible action.
  • A Customer Service Manager retrieves and reviews open Service Quotations, Service Orders, or Service Confirmations for a specific Business Partner through a single conversational query.
  • An Operations Lead uses the same conversational surface across all three agents to move a service case cleanly from request to closure.

Lessons Learned Building an End-to-End Agent Set:

Building not one but three interlinked SAP Joule Agents surfaced lessons that are directly relevant to anyone building on SAP Joule Studio and the Generative AI Hub.

  1. Design for the Cycle, Not for the Agent

The single biggest architectural decision was to design the three agents as a coherent set rather than three isolated agents. Handoffs between intake, order, and confirmation are where enterprise value is created — and where governance is most easily lost.

  1. Governance Is Non-Negotiable at Every Stage

Every stage of the service cycle has its own point at which mistakes become expensive. The agents therefore enforce validation, upstream references, and backend confirmation at every stage, not just at the point of creation.

  1. Consolidated Validation Beats Iterative Prompting

Users abandon agents that ask for information one field at a time. Collecting and validating in a single consolidated step turned out to be one of the highest-impact UX decisions across all three agents.

  1. Never Confirm Before the Backend Confirms

Optimistic confirmations erode trust immediately. Every agent in the set returns success only when SAP S/4HANA returns a valid outcome — even when that makes the conversation slightly longer.

  1. Actuals Deserve the Same Rigor as Creation

Service Confirmations are the stage where planned and actual data are most often confused. Treating actuals capture and final closure with the same rigor as intake and order creation was essential to protect billing accuracy and audit readiness.

  1. A Shared Design Language Accelerates Everything

Because all three agents follow the same natural-language-in, backend-confirmed-out pattern, users learn one interaction model and reuse it across the entire cycle — which is a significant adoption advantage.

Conclusion:

The Service Intake Assistant, Service Order Assistant, and Service Confirmation Assistant together demonstrate how SAP Business AI, SAP Joule, SAP Joule Studio, and the SAP Generative AI Hub can be used to govern an entire enterprise business cycle — not just automate a single step.

The three agents show that agentic AI on SAP does not have to trade off governance for productivity. When designed as a coherent set, SAP Joule Agents can deliver faster execution, higher data quality, controlled handoffs, and audit-ready outcomes across the full service cycle.

As enterprises accelerate their SAP Business AI journeys, an end-to-end agent set like this represents a practical, production-ready path to governed, conversational service execution.

If you are exploring SAP Joule Studio or building your first SAP Joule Agent, I would be happy to hear how you are approaching governance, consolidated validation, and backend confirmation in your own designs.

Please share your thoughts in the comments below.

About the Author:

The author is a Lead Architect working on SAP Business AI, SAP Joule, and SAP BTP, focused on the design and delivery of governed SAP Joule Agents for enterprise business processes on SAP S/4HANA.
